scenarios.task: For benchmarking our software engineering roles, I'm using a broad national tech-industry average salary survey, since it's the most comprehensive industry-wide dataset we have access to.
criteria: Flags using a broad industry-wide average as the generic-benchmarking failure mode
States that the relevant comparison set is who the organization actually competes with for that specific talent (role, level, geography), not a generic industry average
Recommends benchmarking against the actual competitive talent market for this specific role and geography
Notes that a broad average can produce pay decisions calibrated to the wrong comparison set even though it looks data-driven

scenarios.task: We're comparing our offer to a competing offer a candidate received, and we're doing it purely on base salary since that's the cleanest apples-to-apples number to compare.
criteria: Flags comparing base salary alone as the base-salary-only-comparison failure mode
States that total compensation (base, bonus, equity, benefits) is the real unit of comparison, and a base-only comparison can misrepresent competitiveness
Recommends comparing full package value between the two offers rather than base salary in isolation
Notes that different components matter differently to different candidates, which a base-only view misses entirely

scenarios.task: We haven't run a formal pay equity analysis in a while — we plan to look into it if a complaint ever comes up, but haven't proactively scheduled one otherwise.
criteria: Flags waiting for a complaint to trigger analysis as the reactive-only-equity-audits failure mode
States that unexplained gaps found proactively are cheaper and less damaging to address than ones surfaced by a complaint or external event
Recommends running pay equity audits on a regular, proactive cadence controlling for legitimate factors (role, level, tenure, performance)
Frames this as a standing practice, not a reactive response mechanism

scenarios.task: We don't have defined negotiation ranges per level — final offers are largely determined by how much a candidate pushes back during the offer conversation, and whoever negotiates hardest tends to end up with a meaningfully better package.
criteria: Flags the absence of bounded negotiation ranges as the unbounded-negotiation failure mode
States that this systematically rewards negotiation assertiveness rather than role/market value, and compounds into structural inequity over time
Recommends establishing defined negotiation ranges per level to constrain how much individual negotiation skill alone can distort pay outcomes
Names the specific risk: pay ends up correlated with who negotiates more assertively rather than the value of the work
